# Refined AI approach improves noninvasive BCI performance

by Sara Pecchia

Achieving a noteworthy milestone to advance noninvasive brain-controlled interfaces, researchers used AI technology to improve the decoding of human intention and control a continuously moving virtual object all by thinking about it, with unmatched performance.

Pursuing a viable alternative to invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) has been a continued research focus of Carnegie Mellon University’s [He Lab](https://www.cmu.edu/bme/helab/index.html). In 2019, the group used a noninvasive BCI to successfully demonstrate, for the first time, that a mind-controlled robotic arm had the ability to continuously track and follow a computer cursor. As technology has improved, their AI-powered deep learning approach has become more robust and effective. In new work published in *PNAS Nexus*, the group demonstrates that humans can control continuous tracking of a moving object all by thinking about it, with unmatched performance.

Noninvasive BCIs bring a host of advantages, in contrast to their invasive counterparts (think Neuralink or Synchron). These include increased safety, cost-effectiveness, and an ability to be used by numerous patients, as well as the general population. Amid these benefits, however, noninvasive BCIs face challenges because their recordings are less accurate and difficult to interpret.

In He’s [recent study](https://academic.oup.com/pnasnexus/article-lookup/doi/10.1093/pnasnexus/pgae145), a group of 28 human participants were given a complex BCI task to track an object in a two-dimensional space all by thinking about it. During the task, an electroencephalography (EEG) method recorded their activity, from outside the brain. Using AI to train a deep neural network, the He group then directly decoded and interpreted human intentions for continuous object movement using the BCI sensor data. Overall, the work demonstrates the excellent performance of non-invasive BCI for a brain-controlled computerized device.

![A four-part graph showing the EEG BCI, Deep learning, continuous pursuit, and how performance increased with better data](/_files/images/news/2024/0430-em-noninvasive-bci.png)

**Source:** He Lab

“The innovation in AI technology has enabled us to greatly improve the performance versus conventional techniques, and shed light for wide human application in the future,” expressed [Bin He](/directory/bios/he-bin.html), professor of [biomedical engineering](https://www.cmu.edu/bme/) at Carnegie Mellon University.

![A graph with both axes going from -.4 to .4. The cursor and target are moving around as described in the caption.](/_files/images/news/2024/0430-em-2-noninvasive-bci.gif)

**Source:** He Lab

This video shows the cursor and target trajectories throughout a single 60-second trial with lag adjusted. The randomly moving target is represented by a yellow circle, while the cursor that the subject controlled using the BCI system is shown as a blue circle. A human subject controlled the blue cursor using the deep learning-based BCI decoder just by thinking about it.

Moreover, the capability of the group’s AI-powered BCI suggests a direct application to continuously controlling a robotic device.

“We are currently testing this AI-powered noninvasive BCI technology to control sophisticated tasks of a robotic arm,” said He. “Also, we are further testing its applicability to not only able-body subjects, but also stroke patients suffering motor impairments.” In a few years, this may lead to AI-powered assistive robots becoming available to a broad range of potential users.

To this end, motor-impaired patients who are suffering from spinal cord injury, stroke, or other movement impairment, but do not want to receive an implant, stand to benefit immensely from research in this vein. “We keep pushing noninvasive neuroengineering solutions that can help everybody,” added He.

This work was supported in part by the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke, National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health, and National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering.

*Other collaborators on the PNAS Nexus paper include the first authors Dylan Forenzo, BME Ph.D. student, and Hao Zhu, former BME Masters student; Jenn Shanahan, former lab technician; and Jaehyun Lim, BME undergraduate student.*

For media inquiries, please contact Sara Pecchia at [pecchia@cmu.edu](mailto:pecchia@cmu.edu).

Review Article

The history, current state and future possibilities of the non-invasive brain computer interfaces
=================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Frederico Caiado, Arkadiy Ukolov

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •The history of BCI dates back to the 19th century, with the research into EEG. 
*   •EEG is non-invasive, cost-effective, portable, and has high temporal resolution. 
*   •EEG suffers from strong signal degradation through hardware limitations and real-world artifacts. 
*   •New ML algorithms, improved hardware and multimodal devices address these issues. 
*   •EEG BCI solutions show promise in changing both the medical field and everyday life. 

Abstract
--------

This study explores the history and current state of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs), focusing on non-invasive, EEG-based devices. BCIs have evolved from early studies in neurophysiology to real-world applications that convert brain impulses into executable commands. The study discusses the two main categories of BCIs: invasive and non-invasive, highlighting their benefits and limitations. Invasive BCIs provide precise signals but carry higher risks and ethical concerns, while non-invasive BCIs are safer but face challenges with signal deterioration and external noise. The study also evaluates the potential of wider use and availability of non-invasive interfaces by analysing their existing capabilities, limits, and potential future developments. Solutions to overcome technological and ethical challenges are explored to improve usability, user experience, and impact in areas such as healthcare, rehabilitation, entertainment, and cognitive enhancement.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259009352400064X)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000025)

1. Introduction
---------------

Brain-Computer Interfaces represent a significant scientific advancement, a convergence of neuroscience and technology, where a brain (often but not necessarily always human) is connected with external devices. This technology decodes and converts brain impulses into instructions that may be used to operate computers, prosthetic limbs, or other devices, bypassing conventional methods of physical connection and communication such as keyboards, controllers or joysticks. This ability to directly interpret and use the brain's impulses has the potential to introduce remarkable improvements in human capacities, as well as being a novel recovery method for lost functions caused by injury or illness.

It took many decades for BCIs to have evolved from mere theoretical ideas to real-world applications, originating from early studies in neurophysiology and the introduction of electroencephalography (EEG) in the 20th century. These first endeavours to capture and comprehend brain activity were the foundation for the building of interfaces that can convert ideas into executable commands. Followed by advancements in collection devices and methods, signal processing, computer algorithms, and a more profound comprehension of cognitive neuroscience, they brought BCIs from mere experimental stage to tools that have tangible use and application.

BCIs are categorised based on how they interact with the brain: invasive BCIs involve surgically implanted electrodes onto the surface or inside of the brain, providing precise signals but carrying higher risks and ethical concerns; non-invasive BCIs, on the other hand, utilise external sensors such as EEG electrodes to detect brain signals. Despite the greater safety and practicality, non-invasive approaches have challenges with signal deterioration and external noise, which may hinder signal interpretation and application accuracy.

In this article, we investigate the field of BCI technology and its history, providing an insight into the emergence of invasive and non-invasive paradigms, as well as the benefits and limitations of each, through a literature review on the current state of the art in the area, with a particular emphasis on non-invasive, EEG-based devices. This study focuses on evaluating the possibility of the wider use and availability of non-invasive interfaces by analysing their existing capabilities, limits, and potential future developments. It also explores solutions to overcome the technological and ethical challenges of BCIs in order to improve their usability, user experience, and impact in such areas as healthcare, rehabilitation, entertainment, and cognitive enhancement.

By focusing on EEG-based non-invasive BCIs, we limit our study to a specific part of the extensive BCI field. This intentional emphasis enables us to focus our efforts on the exploration of existing possibilities to bring BCI into everyday life in the near future. Nevertheless, further literature reviews considering other collection methods and invasive paradigms, or systematic reviews with a more overhauling and thorough perspective of the field, will be required to have an encompassing view of the full BCI landscape.

2. Historical background
------------------------

### 2.1. General history of EEG

The History of EEG began in the second half of the 19th century, with a publication by Dr Richard Caton in 1875. By applying a galvanometer to grey matter (the outermost layer of the brain) of rabbits and monkeys, he observed the presence of electric currents - which could be claimed to be one of the first observations of the kind [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib1)].

That was followed by a gradually deepening understanding of how electricity was connected to the workings of the brain [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib2)]. It is worth noting three studies performed at the beginning of the 20th century: the first was conducted by Vladimir Vladimirovich Pravdich-Neminsky, who, in 1913, produced the first photograph of the potentials inside an animal's brain; the second, by Hans Berger, who was the first to discover alpha and beta oscillation frequencies in the brain, publishing his results in 1929 (see [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#tbl1) for brain wave ranges) [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib3)].

Table 1. The range of human brainwaves together with their names, frequencies and description (taken from Ref.[[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib5)]).

| Brainwave | Frequency Range | Mental Condition |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Delta | 0–4​Hz | State of deep sleep, when there is no focus, the person is totally absent, uncon- scious. |
| Theta | 4–8​Hz | Deep relaxation, internal focus, meditation, intuition access to unconscious material such as imaging, fantasy, dreaming. |
| Low Alpha High Alpha | 8–10​Hz 10–12​Hz | Wakeful relaxation, consciousness, awareness without attention or concentration, good mood, calmness. Increased self-awareness and focus, learning of new information. |
| Low Beta High Beta | 12–18​Hz 18–30​Hz | Active thinking, active attention, focus towards problem solving, judgment and decision making. Engagement in mental activity, also alertness and agitation. |
| Low Gamma High Gamma | 30–50​Hz 50–70​Hz | Cognitive processing, senses, intelligence, compassion, self-control. Cognitive tasks: memory, hearing, reading and speaking. |

Berger did not pay much attention to those frequencies, believing them to be errors. The waves were later confirmed by following research, but their use was not fully explored until the later half of the 20th century [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib4)].

The third study of interest was conducted in 1947. It introduced the widely-used 10–20 system for EEG measurements, which refers to the placement of electrodes on the scalp, each being either 10 or 20​% of the scalp's distance away from one another, allowing for a comprehensive coverage of the different brain lobes [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib6)].

### 2.2. Non-invasive BCI

Potential for the EEG as an interface between a human brain and a computer first came into light in the 70s, likely promoted by the rapid development of computers. A landmark paper published by Jacques J. Vidal, in 1973, was the first attempt to evaluate the feasibility of using a human brain for the BCI purposes. It justifies the usage of the neocortex as the main source of electric potentials of interest, and proposes to use “waveshapes”, specific waveforms that appear in specific instants, instead of pure frequency analysis, due to their localised nature in time and usefulness for information retrieval [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib7)].

After that, several types of electric potentials and frequencies were tried in the non-invasive BCI experiments. Among are the slow cortical potentials, direct current EEG shifts, event-related potentials and sensorimotor rhythm. Each of these are explored below in some detail.

Slow cortical potentials show when large groups of cortical cells depolarise. That effect was at first used to attempt communication with patients suffering from locked-in syndrome, but has been proven to be less effective than direct-current shifts [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib8)]. Nevertheless, the feedback from slow cortical potentials is still used in different areas, such as treatments of children with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib9)].

Direct current shifts are, as the name suggests, sudden changes in the direction of the current. They were first observed by W.G. Walter et al., in 1964. Their nature is still obscure, but since Walter's work they have been associated with the different pathophysiological conditions such as epileptic seizures, cerebral hypoxia and spreading depolarization [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib11)].

Event-related potentials are generated when the brain responds to stimuli or events. Said events could be sensory, motor or cognitive. They provided a convenient way to study mental processes in a non-invasive way [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib12)]. The BCI-related studies started from a research by L A. Farwell and E. Donchin, wherein they displayed letters of the alphabet on a screen and recorded brain activity of subjects. By asking them to focus on specific letters they wished to communicate, Farwell and Donchin managed to extract distinct signals, thereby proving the possibility of using these potentials to communicate with patients who cannot express themselves via other means (e.g. locked-in patients) [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib13)].

The final mode of interest is the sensorimotor rhythm, also known as a mu-rhythm. It is a distinct brain wave which is associated with movement in ways not yet fully understood. It normally occurs at the frequencies of 7–11​Hz, which means it can sometimes be confused with an alpha wave due to the overlapping frequencies and much higher intensity of the latter [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib14)]. In 1991, J.R. Wolpaw et al. conducted a study in which subjects learned to change the amplitude of their mu-rhythm to manipulate the motion of a cursor on a screen. Over the course of several weeks, patients succeeded in achieving 1D movements (up and down) [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib10)]. More than a decade later, J.R. Wolpaw et al. finally succeeded in obtaining 2D movements by extending their scans to encompass beta wave readings as well [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib15)].

Much thanks to the rapidly developing field of computer science, the emergence of fast data processing algorithms and superior computers allowed for the rapid development of the non-invasive EEG-based BCIs in the 21st century, starting from the proposition by Dennis J. McFarland et al. of a 64-channel EEG system with sophisticated filtering algorithms, a setup that has since been widely used in the field [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib16)].

One article of note is that of José del R. Millán _et aI_, which could be considered one of the first uses of a neural network for the classification purposes of the EEG signals. The researchers trained a single 10-neuron fully-connected layer on the data gathered from five patients and obtained satisfactory results for the right movement tasks (75–100​%) but less satisfactory for the left movement tasks (53–61​%) [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib17)].

That was followed by Benjamin Blankertz et al., who also used a machine learning approach for the signal extraction. The researchers used a 128-channel EEG headset and a neural network of an unspecified architecture, which applied an algorithm called common spatial pattern [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib18)]. They were successful, with an average accuracy rate of the signal classification being 88​%. However, their approach demanded to fine-tune their network to each participant, a procedure that required gathering individual data and, by implication, means that it cannot be generally extended without EEG calibration [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib19)].

In more recent years, more sophisticated networks were used for the EEG classification. As an example, a study done by John Thomas et al. applied a convolutional network (1 convolutional 32x32 layer with a 3000-strong full-connected output layer) to analyse 30-min recordings of 156 subjects, half of which suffered from epilepsy. They achieved an accuracy of 83.86​% in recognising epilepsy symptoms from the EEG signals [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib20)].

In spite of the fact that they have been widely used in computer vision, fMRI image scans, and tumour detection, deep neural networks are only beginning to be used for the BCI analysis. The state of the art could be highlighted by a 2023 paper by Farheen Siddiqui et al., where a 3-layered fully-connected network with varying numbers of neurons (12–24) was applied to distinguish between right-hand and left-hand movements of three subjects. Depending on the number of neurons, the accuracy fluctuated between 77​% and 80​% [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib21)].

### 2.3. Invasive BCI

In parallel to the non-invasive studies, invasive BCIs were being actively researched. The very first BCI implant was done by P.R. Kennedy and R.A. Bakay in 1998. The researchers inserted electrodes under the scalp of a paralysed woman, who, after many months of training, was able to send on/off signals, paving the way for the invasive BCI research [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib22)].

A year later, in 1999, Birbaumer, N. et al. used an invasive device to read slow cortical potentials as a means of communication between paralysed patients and a computer that served as a “spelling device” [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib23)].

In 2004, a quadriplegic man had been implanted with a device that allowed him to freely move a cursor on a screen, switch lights and TV channels, and read emails - a feat of control as of yet unreached by non-invasive approaches. That study, however, required the device to be placed directly into the patient's motor cortex, an operation that is still considered to be a risky enterprise [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib24)].

A slightly less invasive solution was proposed in 2006 by Eric C Leuthardt et al., demonstrating the efficiency of electrocorticography, in which electrodes are placed onto the surface of the cortex yet not inserted into it. In the 1D cursor motions, their patients achieved 73–100​% success rate, proving the efficacy of their approach, coupled with the power and flexibility of this source of signals [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib25)].

An important contribution was made by Leigh R. Hochberg et al. in 2012. The researchers implanted two human tetraplegic subjects with a 96-channel microelectrode array. The array was inserted into their motor cortex area that was responsible for hand movements. After several years of training and studies, the patients learned to control a robotic hand [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib26)]. Despite the reach and grasp of the artificial hand being inferior compared to able-bodied motions, that approach proved the possibility of using the invasive BCI for tetraplegic patients.

The state-of-the-art of the invasive BCI could be illuminated by a 2017 research by A Bolu Ajiboye et al. Patients with tetraplegia were implanted with electrode arrays into their motor cortex, and, after several months, had more electrodes inserted into their upper and lower arm. They learned to electrically stimulate those muscles to regain partial control of their arm, with an impressive 80–100​% success rate of meeting the grasping/movement targets [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib27)].

Nevertheless, despite the admirable successes of the invasive BCIs, such operations always carry a certain degree of risk. The main one is perhaps the natural defence mechanisms of the human body, a problem that is still not solved: the human body can reject the implant as it considers it to be an invading, foreign entity, hence rendering the entire apparatus entirely unworkable. In addition to that, invasive BCIs involve, as the name suggests, an invasive surgery, which can lead to damaged cells and blood vessels, leading to a risk of infection [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib28)].

3. Collection devices
---------------------

The signal of choice and its collection method depend on the application and the context of the BCI. In broad terms, the neural interface can be classified as invasive and non-invasive, as described in Section [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#sec2) [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib3),[[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib28), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib29), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib30)].

When it comes to invasive BCIs, the collection methods encompass Electrocorticogram (ECoG) and intracortical recordings [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib29)]. Both of these methods require the implantation of the respective device, and therefore surgical intervention.

In the case of the ECoG, the device consists of a membrane superficially placed on the cortex (on the surface or below the dura mater, see [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#fig1) A)), with a variable number of electrodes. These electrons measure cortical electrical activity without any ocular, muscular, cardiac, environmental or movement artifacts [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib31),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib32)].

![Image 8: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590093525000049-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (383KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590093525000049-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (383KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590093525000049-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.1. Radiography images of the implanted electrodes. A), ECoG and micro ECoG (μECoG) arrays [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib32)]. B), eight depth intracranial electrodes [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib34)].

In the intracortical electrode case, such as the one represented in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#fig1) B), their readings, generally cortical as well, are further spatially resolved due to their proximity to the neurons. The precision could be as high as measuring the activity of a single neuron [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib33)].

When it comes to non-invasive BCIs, the collection methods depend on the apparatus in question. Possible options are MRI, fNIRS or EEG, or a hybrid combination of those.

The fMRI (functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging; see [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#fig2) (a)) measures the level of oxygen in the brain, inferring from that the level of neural activity. The fMRI has the highest spatial resolutions among the non-invasive BCIs, providing a rich amount of detail [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib35),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#bib36)]. It also allows for a full-brain scan, while EEG only takes measurements of the surface area of the brain. Its disadvantages, on the other hand, include the significant investment cost (each MRI costs hundreds of thousands of dollars, plus the high cost of running the device), lack of portability due to the bulkiness of the machine, and complexity of usage. These constitute a hurdle for fMRI as a BCI system for regular use.

![Image 9: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590093525000049-gr2.jpg)

Similar problems are faced by Magnetoencephalography (MEG) and Positron Emitting Tomography (PET) (see [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590093525000049#fig2), (b) and (c)). The former measures the magnetic field induced by the electr

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in AI have significantly enhanced non-invasive BCIs, improving their accuracy and usability while ensuring safety and accessibility for a broader range of users.

The integration of AI and machine learning enables better interpretation of brain signals, leading to more effective control of devices and potential applications in rehabilitation and assistive technologies.

Emerging BCI technologies hold promise for personalized applications, allowing tailored solutions for individual users, particularly in healthcare and therapeutic settings for motor-impaired patients.
